mnemonics.title: Data Type Sizes
mnemonics.content: I Intend To Make Great Contributions - int(4), float(4), double(8), char(1), bool(1)

mnemonics.title: Operator Precedence
mnemonics.content: My Dear Aunt Sally - Multiplication/Division, Exponents (not in C++), Addition/Subtraction. Parentheses override all.

mnemonics.title: Increment/Decrement
mnemonics.content: Pre-Pete (++i) comes before, Post-Pete (i++) comes after. Pre gets new value, Post gets old value.

acronyms.expansion: Plain Old Data - basic data types like int, float, char without complex structures
acronyms.usage: Used to categorize simple data types in C++ for memory operations

acronyms.expansion: American Standard Code for Information Interchange
acronyms.usage: Character encoding where 'A'=65, 'a'=97, '0'=48 used by char type

acronyms.expansion: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers
acronyms.usage: Standard for float/double representation (sign, exponent, mantissa)
Compiler optimization that avoids copying return values

visualTips.visualization: Draw 10 circles, group them in sets of 4. You get 2 full groups (quotient=2) and 2 remaining (remainder=2)
Each bit is like a light switch - either ON (1) or OFF (0). 8 bits = 1 byte, shown as 8 switches in a row.
Variable is a house, pointer is the address written on paper. & gives the address, * opens the house door.
Think of true as light ON, false as light OFF. AND needs both ON, OR is any ON, NOT flips the switch.

analogies.analogy: Like containers - int is a small box for whole numbers, double is a bigger container for decimal numbers, char is for single letters.
Variables are named boxes. The name is on the outside (identifier), the value is inside. You can change what's inside.
Operators are verbs that tell computers what to do. + adds, == checks if equal, && requires both conditions to be true.
Like converting currency. Sometimes you lose precision (dollars to rupees, decimal part dropped), sometimes you gain precision (int to double).
